docs.title: Rooted
docs.text:
A living knowledge base documenting my journey into cybersecurity.

Built through hands-on labs, deliberate study, and reflection over time.

  • Roadmap

    Where I am, where I've been, and where I'm going.

    View roadmap

  • Notes

    Foundational concepts I revisit and refine as I learn.

    Browse notes

  • Walkthroughs

    Writeups from TryHackMe, HackTheBox, and CTFs — focused on understanding attack paths.

    See writeups

  • Tools

    Commands and cheatsheets for tools I actually use.

    Browse tools

  • Methodology

    My personal attack methodology and recon frameworks.

    View methodology

  • Reflections

    Mistakes and lessons that only show up after repetition.

    Read reflections

docs.title: About
docs.text:
Rooted is my attempt to learn cybersecurity deliberately.

Instead of scattered notes and forgotten writeups, this site acts as a long-term record of what I study, what I struggle with, and what eventually clicks.

The goal isn't just to "get root" — but to build intuition around why systems fail and how they can be defended.

Heads up

This is a living document. Notes are rough, things change as my understanding improves, and some pages are still being built out.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
My personal approach to each phase of a pentest or CTF — built from repetition and refined as I get better.

Living document

This evolves as I learn. Early entries will be rough — that's fine.

  • Recon & Enumeration

    Information gathering before touching anything — passive and active recon, port scanning, and service enumeration.

    Recon methodology

  • Web App Testing

    My approach to testing web applications — from spidering to exploiting common vulnerabilities.

    Web app methodology

  • Network Pentesting

    Attacking network services — SMB, FTP, SSH, and beyond.

    Network methodology

  • Privilege Escalation

    Getting from low-privileged user to root or SYSTEM — Linux and Windows.

    PrivEsc methodology

  • Active Directory

    Attacking AD environments — enumeration, Kerberoasting, lateral movement, and domain dominance.

    AD methodology

  • Malware Analysis

    Static and dynamic analysis — understanding what malware does and how it does it.

    Malware analysis methodology

docs.title: The General Flow
docs.text:
Recon → Enumeration → Foothold → Post-Exploitation → Privilege Escalation → Persistence

Every engagement starts with recon and ends with documentation. The steps in between depend on what you find — but the mindset stays the same: understand before you act.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Everything I've learned, organized by where I learned it.

These aren't polished guides — they're my working understanding at a given point in time. Expect rough edges, corrections, and updates as my knowledge improves.

  • TryHackMe

    Notes from rooms and learning paths on TryHackMe.

    TryHackMe notes

  • HackTheBox

    Notes from HackTheBox machines and challenges.

    HackTheBox notes

  • YouTube / Courses

    Notes from YouTube videos, online courses, and tutorials.

    Course notes

  • Personal Research

    Notes from rabbit holes, self-directed research, and experiments.

    Research notes

  • Books / Reading

    Notes and highlights from books and long-form reading.

    Book notes

  • Coding

    Python, C, C++ and more — programming skills that make you a better hacker.

    Coding notes

How notes are structured

Each source section is broken down by topic or room. So if you're looking for networking fundamentals from TryHackMe, you'd find it under TryHackMe → Networking Fundamentals.

docs.title: What is Networking?
docs.text:
Networking is simply things connected. Just like a friend circle, where everyone is connected because they share similar interests or hobbies. A network can range from two devices to tens or thousands of devices.

Platform: TryHackMe Path: Pre-Security → Network Fundamentals Difficulty: Easy Status: ✅ Completed

docs.title: What This Room Covers
docs.text: Covers what a network is, what the internet is, how devices are identified using IP and MAC addresses, and how to use the ping command.

docs.title: What is a Network?
docs.text: Networking is simply things connected. Just like a friend circle, where everyone is connected because they share similar interests or hobbies. A network can range from two devices to tens or thousands of devices.

docs.title: What is the Internet?
docs.text:
The Internet is a vast network made up of numerous smaller groups of interconnected devices.

There are two types of networks: - Private networks — restricted, e.g. your home or office network - Public networks — the internet

docs.title: IP Addresses
docs.text:
An IP (Internet Protocol) address is how a device is identified on a network. It's made up of 4 sections (octets), each being 8 bits, giving a range of 0–255 per section.

192.168.1.1
Type Description Public IP Identifies your device on the internet — assigned by your ISP Private IP Identifies your device on a local network — not routable on the internet

Common private IP ranges: 

10.0.0.0    – 10.255.255.255
172.16.0.0  – 172.31.255.255
192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255

docs.title: MAC Addresses
docs.text:
A MAC (Media Access Control) address is a physical identifier burned into a device's network interface card (NIC) at the factory. Unlike IP addresses, MAC addresses are hardware-level. So it can’t be changed.

a4:c3:f0:85:ac:2d
  • First 6 characters = manufacturer identifier
  • Last 6 characters = unique device identifier
  • Can be spoofed in software despite being "physical"

MAC Spoofing

The interactive lab in this room shows exactly why MAC addresses aren't a reliable security measure — Bob spoofs Alice's MAC address to bypass a router that uses MAC filtering to control internet access. This is a real attack technique.

docs.title: Ping
docs.text:
Ping is a basic network diagnostic tool that uses ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) packets to test whether a connection between two devices exists and how reliable it is.

ping 10.10.10.10        # ping an IP address
ping google.com         # ping a domain
ping -c 4 10.10.10.10   # send only 4 packets (Linux)

The output shows the round-trip time (RTT) in milliseconds — how long a packet took to travel to the target and back.

docs.title: Commands & Syntax
docs.text:
# Basic ping
ping <IP or domain>

# Limit number of packets (Linux/Mac)
ping -c 4 10.10.10.10

# Limit number of packets (Windows)
ping -n 4 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Notes & Things I Learned
docs.text:
• A network is just devices connected together. Either it could be just two devices or thousands, if not millions.
  • The internet was technically created in the 1960s (ARPANET) but the WWW wasn't invented until 1989 by Tim Berners-Lee
  • IP addresses identify devices logically — they can change
  • MAC addresses identify devices physically — they're tied to the hardware but can still be spoofed
  • Ping uses ICMP — not TCP or UDP
  • MAC filtering is a weak security control because MAC addresses can be spoofed

docs.title: Questions & Answers
docs.text:
What is the key term for devices that are connected together?

Network

Who invented the World Wide Web?

Tim Berners-Lee

What does the term 'IP' stand for?

Internet Protocol

What is each section of an IP address called?

Octet

How many sections does an IPv4 address have?

4

What does the term 'MAC' stand for?

Media Access Control

[Interactive Lab] Deploy the interactive lab and spoof your MAC address to access the site. What is the flag?

THM{YOU_GOT_ON_TRYHACKME}

What protocol does ping use?

ICMP

What is the syntax to ping 10.10.10.10?

Ping 10.10.10.10

[Interactive Lab] What flag do you get when you ping 8.8.8.8?

THM{I_PINGED_THE_SERVER}

docs.text:
• TryHackMe — What is Networking? room
  • TryHackMe — Pre-Security Path
  • Wikipedia — ARPANET

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Networks are connected devices. The internet is a massive public network made up of smaller networks. Devices are identified by IP addresses (logical, can change) and MAC addresses (physical, tied to hardware but can be spoofed). Ping uses ICMP to test connectivity between devices.

docs.title: Reflections
docs.text:
Mistakes and lessons that only show up after repetition.

Quote

"If you’re going hard enough left, you’ll find yourself turning right. - Smokey (aka: breaking things in cybersecurity is often how you learn to secure them)

Quote

"Everyone has a plan until they get punched in the mouth." — Mike Tyson (also applicable to CTFs)

docs.title: How This Section Works
docs.text:
Each entry is a short reflection — something I got wrong, something that finally clicked, or something I keep having to relearn. No polish, just honest notes.

Reflections will be added here as the site grows.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
My learning path broken down by discipline — from complete beginner to pro.

  • Red Team

    Offensive security — enumeration, exploitation, post-exploitation, Active Directory, and beyond.

    Red Team roadmap

  • Blue Team

    Defensive security — log analysis, SIEM, network traffic analysis, incident response, and threat intel.

    Blue Team roadmap

  • Purple Team

    Where offense meets defense — MITRE ATT&CK, adversary emulation, detection engineering, and threat hunting.

    Purple Team roadmap

Where to start

If you're a complete beginner, I'd suggest you should start with the Red Team roadmap — it covers the foundations that both blue and purple teaming build on top of.

docs.title: Blue Team Roadmap
docs.text:
The defensive side — learning to detect, investigate, and respond to attacks.

This is my Roadmap for Blue Teaming

Blue team work is less glamorous than red but arguably more important. Work through each phase in order. The later phases assume you understand how attacks work, so pair this with the red team roadmap.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Foundations
docs.text: You need to understand what normal looks like before you can spot abnormal.

docs.title: Operating System Basics
docs.text:
• Windows fundamentals (registry, services, event logs, user accounts)
  • Linux fundamentals (syslog, auth.log, cron, processes)
  • File system structure on both OSes
  • How processes and services work

docs.title: Networking Fundamentals
docs.text:
• TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP/S
  • How to read a packet capture
  • Common attack traffic patterns (port scans, brute force, beaconing)
  • Firewalls, proxies, and how traffic is filtered

Resources: - TryHackMe — Pre-Security Path - TryHackMe — SOC Level 1 Path

docs.title: Phase 2 — Log Analysis
docs.text:
Logs are the blue teamer's best friend. Learn to read them.

  • Windows Event Logs — key Event IDs to know:

    Event ID Meaning 4624 Successful logon 4625 Failed logon 4648 Logon with explicit credentials 4672 Special privileges assigned 4688 New process created 4698 Scheduled task created 7045 New service installed

  • Linux logs (/var/log/auth.log, /var/log/syslog, journalctl)

  • Web server logs (Apache/Nginx access logs)
  • Firewall logs
  • Identifying suspicious patterns in logs manually

Resources: - TryHackMe — Windows Event Logs room - YouTube — John Hammond log analysis videos

docs.title: Phase 3 — Network Traffic Analysis
docs.text:
Learn to read what's moving across the wire.

  • Wireshark basics (filters, following streams, exporting objects)
  • Identifying normal vs suspicious traffic
  • Detecting port scans in packet captures
  • Detecting reverse shells in traffic
  • DNS exfiltration basics
  • HTTP vs HTTPS — what you can and can't see
  • Zeek / Suricata basics (IDS/IPS)

Resources: - TryHackMe — Wireshark rooms - TryHackMe — Network Security module

docs.title: Phase 4 — SIEM
docs.text:
Centralizing logs so you can actually find things at scale.

  • What a SIEM is and why it exists
  • Splunk basics:
    • Ingesting logs
    • Search Processing Language (SPL) basics
    • Building dashboards
    • Creating alerts
  • Elastic Stack basics (alternative to Splunk):
    • Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana (ELK)
    • KQL (Kibana Query Language)
  • Correlation rules — what they are and how to write them
  • Alert fatigue — tuning out false positives

Resources: - TryHackMe — Splunk rooms - Splunk Free Training - YouTube — MyDFIR Splunk tutorials

docs.title: Phase 5 — Endpoint Detection
docs.text:
What's happening on the machine itself.

  • What EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) is
  • Sysmon — what it logs and why it matters:
    • Process creation (Event ID 1)
    • Network connections (Event ID 3)
    • File creation (Event ID 11)
    • Registry changes (Event ID 13)
  • Deploying Sysmon with a hardened config (SwiftOnSecurity config)
  • Hunting with Sysmon logs in a SIEM
  • AV evasion from a defender's perspective (what attackers do to bypass AV)

Resources: - TryHackMe — Sysmon room - GitHub — SwiftOnSecurity Sysmon config

docs.title: Phase 6 — Threat Intelligence & MITRE ATT&CK
docs.text:
Understanding adversary behavior at a higher level.

  • What threat intelligence is (strategic, tactical, operational)
  • MITRE ATT&CK framework:
    • Tactics vs Techniques vs Sub-techniques
    • How to navigate the ATT&CK matrix
    • Mapping real attacks to ATT&CK
  • IOCs (Indicators of Compromise) — hashes, IPs, domains
  • Threat intel platforms (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, AlienVault OTX)
  • OSINT for defenders

Resources: - MITRE ATT&CK - TryHackMe — Cyber Threat Intelligence module

docs.title: Phase 7 — Incident Response
docs.text:
Something got through. Now what?

  • The IR lifecycle (Preparation → Detection → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned)
  • Triage — determining scope and severity quickly
  • Memory forensics basics (Volatility)
  • Disk forensics basics (Autopsy, FTK Lite)
  • Timeline analysis
  • Writing an incident report

Resources: - TryHackMe — SOC Level 1 Path - YouTube — 13Cubed forensics channel

docs.title: Certifications (when ready)
docs.text: Cert Level Notes CompTIA Security+ Beginner Solid blue team theory baseline CompTIA CySA+ Mid Analyst-focused, highly relevant Blue Team Labs Online Beginner–Mid Practical, free tier available BTL1 (Blue Team Labs Level 1) Mid Hands-on, well respected GCIH (GIAC) Advanced Incident handling, expensive but respected

docs.title: Purple Team Roadmap
docs.text:
Where offense meets defense — using both sides to make each other better.

Prerequisites

Don't start here. Complete at least Phase 1–3 of the Red Team roadmap and Phase 1–4 of the Blue Team roadmap first. Purple teaming only makes sense once you have context on both sides.

docs.title: What Purple Teaming Actually Is
docs.text:
Red teams attack. Blue teams defend. Purple teams run the whole cycle deliberately:

Plan attack → Execute attack → Observe what was detected → 
Tune detections → Re-run attack → Repeat

The goal isn't just to compromise a system — it's to build better detections by understanding exactly how attacks behave and what telemetry they leave behind.

docs.title: Phase 1 — MITRE ATT&CK Deep Dive
docs.text:
The common language that red and blue both speak.

  • Understand the full ATT&CK matrix structure (14 tactics)
  • Learn key techniques in each tactic:
    • Reconnaissance (T1595, T1596)
    • Initial Access (T1190, T1566 — phishing)
    • Execution (T1059 — command line, scripting)
    • Persistence (T1053 — scheduled tasks, T1547 — registry run keys)
    • Privilege Escalation (T1548, T1055)
    • Defense Evasion (T1070, T1027 — obfuscation)
    • Credential Access (T1003 — credential dumping, T1110 — brute force)
    • Lateral Movement (T1021 — remote services)
    • Exfiltration (T1041, T1048)
    • Command & Control (T1071 — standard protocols)
  • Use ATT&CK Navigator to map and track techniques
  • Map a real-world attack (e.g. a known APT) to ATT&CK techniques

Resources: - MITRE ATT&CK - ATT&CK Navigator - TryHackMe — MITRE room

docs.title: Phase 2 — Adversary Emulation
docs.text:
Running controlled, documented attacks to test your defenses.

  • Understand what adversary emulation is vs a standard pentest
  • Atomic Red Team:
    • What it is (pre-built attack simulations mapped to ATT&CK)
    • Installing and running Atomic tests
    • Running a test and checking if your SIEM caught it
  • MITRE Caldera:
    • Setting up Caldera
    • Running an automated emulation plan
    • Reviewing what was and wasn't detected
  • Manually emulating a technique and documenting the telemetry it produces

Resources: - Atomic Red Team - MITRE Caldera - YouTube — John Hammond adversary emulation videos

docs.title: Phase 3 — Detection Engineering
docs.text:
Writing rules that actually catch attacks.

  • What detection engineering is
  • Sigma rules — the universal detection format:
    • Reading existing Sigma rules
    • Writing your own Sigma rule from scratch
    • Converting Sigma to Splunk SPL or KQL
  • Writing Splunk detections based on Sysmon telemetry
  • Understand the Pyramid of Pain (why IOCs are weak, behaviors are strong)
  • Baselining — what does normal look like so anomalies stand out
  • Reducing false positives through tuning

Resources: - Sigma Rules GitHub - TryHackMe — Sigma room - YouTube — MyDFIR detection engineering

docs.title: Phase 4 — Threat Hunting
docs.text:
Proactively looking for attackers who got past your detections.

  • What threat hunting is (hypothesis-driven investigation)
  • The threat hunting loop:
    1. Form a hypothesis (e.g. "there may be lateral movement via WMI")
    2. Collect relevant data
    3. Investigate and look for anomalies
    4. If found → escalate. If not → document and improve detections
  • Hunting with Sysmon + Splunk/Elastic
  • Common hunting hypotheses:
    • Unusual parent-child process relationships
    • PowerShell downloading files from the internet
    • Encoded commands in process arguments
    • Outbound connections on unusual ports
    • Accounts logging in at unusual hours
  • Building a threat hunting playbook

Resources: - TryHackMe — Threat Hunting module - PEAK Threat Hunting Framework

docs.title: Phase 5 — Running a Full Purple Team Exercise
docs.text:
Putting it all together.

  • Plan a purple team exercise:
    • Pick an ATT&CK technique to emulate
    • Define what "detected" looks like
    • Define what telemetry you expect to see
  • Execute the attack in your lab
  • Check your SIEM — was it detected?
  • If yes → document the detection, move to the next technique
  • If no → write a new detection rule, re-run, verify it fires
  • Document everything in a purple team report:
    • Technique tested
    • Attack executed
    • Detection result (caught / missed)
    • Rule written (if applicable)
    • Residual risk

docs.title: Home Lab for Purple Teaming
docs.text:
Minimum setup:

Attacker VM (Kali Linux)
    ↓ attacks
Target VM (Windows 10)
    ↓ logs forwarded to
SIEM VM (Splunk or Elastic)
  • Set up Kali Linux VM
  • Set up Windows 10 VM
  • Install and configure Splunk or Elastic
  • Forward Sysmon logs to your SIEM
  • Run your first Atomic Red Team test and verify it shows up in the SIEM

Resources: - YouTube — MyDFIR home lab series (best purple team lab guide out there) - Splunk Free (500MB/day)

docs.title: Certifications (when ready)
docs.text: Cert Level Notes eJPT Beginner Good red foundation before going purple BTL1 Mid Blue team practical, pairs well PNPT Mid Offensive practical by TCM Security CPTIA Mid Purple team specific cert OSCP Advanced Advanced offensive — long-term goal

docs.title: Red Team Roadmap
docs.text:
The offensive side — learning to think like an attacker.

This is my Roadmap for Red Teaming

Work through each phase in order. Don't skip ahead — the later phases assume the earlier ones. Check off items as you go and link to your notes/writeups as you build them.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Absolute Basics
docs.text: Getting comfortable with the fundamentals before touching anything offensive.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
• Navigating the file system (cd, ls, pwd, find)
  • Reading and writing files (cat, nano, echo, >, >>)
  • File permissions (chmod, chown, ls -la)
  • Users and groups (whoami, id, su, sudo)
  • Processes (ps, kill, top)
  • Networking commands (ip a, ping, curl, wget)

Resources: - TryHackMe — Linux Fundamentals (Parts 1, 2, 3) - YouTube — NetworkChuck Linux for Hackers

docs.title: Networking
docs.text:
• OSI model (what each layer does)
  • TCP vs UDP
  • IP addressing & subnetting basics
  • Common ports and protocols (22, 80, 443, 445, 3389...)
  • DNS, HTTP/S basics
  • How a packet travels across a network

Resources: - TryHackMe — Pre-Security Path - YouTube — Professor Messer CompTIA Network+

docs.title: Phase 2 — Recon & Enumeration
docs.text:
You can't attack what you don't understand. Recon is everything.

  • Passive recon (OSINT, Shodan, Google dorking)
  • Active recon (Nmap — host discovery, port scanning, service detection)
  • Web enumeration (Gobuster, Nikto, directory busting)
  • SMB enumeration (enum4linux, smbclient, smbmap)
  • DNS enumeration (dig, nslookup, zone transfers)
  • Subdomain discovery

Resources: - TryHackMe — Jr Penetration Tester Path - TryHackMe — Nmap room

docs.title: Phase 3 — Exploitation Basics
docs.text:
Finding vulnerabilities and getting that first shell.

  • Understanding CVEs and vulnerability databases (NVD, ExploitDB)
  • Manual exploitation basics
  • Metasploit framework (search, use, set options, run)
  • Password attacks (Hydra, Medusa, brute forcing login panels)
  • Web vulnerabilities:
    • SQL Injection (manual + sqlmap)
    • XSS (reflected, stored)
    • Command injection
    • File upload vulnerabilities
    • LFI / RFI
  • Reverse shells & bind shells (Netcat, PHP, Python)

Resources: - TryHackMe — OWASP Top 10 room - TryHackMe — Metasploit module - PortSwigger Web Security Academy (free, excellent)

docs.title: Phase 4 — Post-Exploitation
docs.text:
You're in. Now what?

  • Situational awareness (who am I, what's on this machine, what network is this)
  • Linux privilege escalation:
    • Sudo misconfigurations (sudo -l)
    • SUID binaries
    • Cron jobs
    • Writable /etc/passwd
    • Kernel exploits
  • Windows privilege escalation:
    • Unquoted service paths
    • Weak service permissions
    • Token impersonation
    • AlwaysInstallElevated
  • Persistence techniques
  • Credential dumping (Mimikatz basics)
  • Transferring files to/from target

Resources: - TryHackMe — Linux PrivEsc room - TryHackMe — Windows PrivEsc room - GTFOBins (essential reference) - YouTube — TCM Security PrivEsc courses

docs.title: Phase 5 — Active Directory
docs.text:
Most real-world environments run AD. This is where it gets serious.

  • What Active Directory is and how it works
  • Enumeration (BloodHound, PowerView, net commands)
  • Kerberoasting
  • AS-REP Roasting
  • Pass-the-Hash / Pass-the-Ticket
  • DCSync attack
  • Golden & Silver Tickets

Resources: - TryHackMe — Active Directory Basics - HackTheBox — Starting Point machines - YouTube — TCM Security Practical Ethical Hacking course

docs.title: Phase 6 — Real Boxes & CTFs
docs.text:
Stop learning, start doing.

  • Complete 25 TryHackMe rooms
  • Complete 5 HackTheBox easy machines
  • Complete 5 HackTheBox medium machines
  • Participate in at least one CTF
  • Write a walkthrough for every box you complete

docs.title: Certifications (when ready)
docs.text: Cert Level Notes CompTIA Security+ Beginner Good baseline, mostly theory eJPT Beginner–Mid Practical, great first offensive cert PNPT Mid TCM Security, highly practical OSCP Advanced The gold standard for pentesters

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Commands, flags, and cheatsheets for tools I actually use. Each page covers basic usage, common flags, and real examples.

docs.title: Reconnaissance & Enumeration
docs.text:
• Nmap

    Network scanner — host discovery, port scanning, service detection.

    Nmap cheatsheet

  • Gobuster

    Directory and DNS brute-forcer.

    Gobuster cheatsheet

docs.title: Web Application Testing
docs.text:
• Burp Suite

    Web proxy for intercepting, modifying, and replaying HTTP requests.

    Burp Suite notes

  • SQLmap

    Automated SQL injection detection and exploitation.

    SQLmap cheatsheet

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
• Metasploit

    Exploitation framework — finding, validating, and exploiting vulnerabilities.

    Metasploit notes

  • Netcat

    The Swiss Army knife — port scanning, file transfer, reverse shells.

    Netcat cheatsheet

  • Hydra

    Fast network login cracker — brute forcing services.

    Hydra cheatsheet

docs.title: Password Cracking
docs.text:
• Hashcat

    GPU-accelerated password cracker — the fastest hash cracker out there.

    Hashcat cheatsheet

  • John the Ripper

    Classic password cracker — great for quick cracks and CTFs.

    John cheatsheet

docs.title: Network Analysis
docs.text:
• Wireshark

    Packet capture and analysis — see exactly what's on the wire.

    Wireshark notes

More tools coming

This list grows as I learn new tools. Check back regularly.

docs.title: Walkthroughs
docs.text:
Writeups from hands-on labs — focused on understanding the why behind each step, not just the commands.

Spoiler policy

These writeups contain full solutions. If you want to try a box first, do that before reading.

  • TryHackMe

    Guided rooms and learning paths.

    TryHackMe writeups

  • HackTheBox

    Retired machines and challenges.

    HackTheBox writeups

  • CTF

    Competition writeups.

    CTF writeups
